Title: Terpene metabolic engineering via nuclear or chloroplast genomes profoundly and globally impacts off-target pathways through metabolite signalling

Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in nuclear transgenic line; Chloroplast in transplastomic line
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: WT 1068 introduction; Petit Havana (background referenced for SQS-only transplastomic comparison)
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Prenyl diphosphate supply step upstream of squalene formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses isopentenyl diphosphate and dimethylallyl diphosphate to produce farnesyl diphosphate, the direct substrate for squalene synthase
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies immediate precursor pool for target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; precursor-supply step limited by substrate/storage context
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Industrial/high-value triterpene; study focuses on engineering output rather than endogenous physiological role
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; likely glandular trichomes discussed as sinks
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expressed in engineered leaves; highest transcript abundance in transplastomic C and CN contexts for chloroplast-expressed constructs
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: N line: NA in this paper; C line plastid transgene driven by Prrn with T7 gene10 5' translational control element and tobacco rbcL 3' UTR
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plants compared under sterile tissue culture via nodal cutting; third youngest leaf harvested at identical developmental stage
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic/transplastomic expression; northern blot for chloroplast FPS transcript; metabolite profiling by GC/MS and UHPLC/MS/MS; phenotyping; RNA-seq
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (GEO: GSE74103); metabolomics by GC/MS and UHPLC/MS/MS
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Abnormal leaf and flower development, reduced pollen and fertility, broad off-target metabolome and transcriptome perturbation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: When co-expressed with SQS, increases squalene accumulation to ~150-fold over WT but does not further raise squalene above N despite much higher transplastomic expression; contributes to global off-target metabolic effects
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FPS was engineered with SQS from nuclear and/or chloroplast genomes to promote squalene biosynthesis in tobacco. 2. Chloroplast-expressed FPS transcript accumulated strongly, yet squalene accumulation plateaued at about 150-fold over WT, indicating substrate or storage limitation. 3. FPS/SQS engineering caused severe developmental phenotypes and extensive off-target metabolomic and transcriptomic changes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful with SQS for boosting squalene, but should be paired with strategies that expand precursor supply and sink/storage capacity; avoid simple overexpression alone because of strong pleiotropic effects
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Source gene was a codon-optimized 4xHis-tagged avian FPS; discussion links FPS demand to MEP-pathway precursor imbalance and possible retrograde signalling

Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast-targeted in nuclear transgenic line; Chloroplast in transplastomic line
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: WT 1068 introduction; Petit Havana (SQS-only transplastomic comparison)
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as sufficient alone; very high protein accumulation did not increase squalene beyond N because of substrate/storage limitations
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Industrial/high-value triterpene; in this study mainly target engineered metabolite
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glandular trichomes proposed as likely sinks
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Flag-SQS protein detected in N, C and CN; ~1400–2300-fold higher in C and ~2800–4372-fold higher in CN than N
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: C/CN plastid construct: tobacco psbA promoter with 5' UTR and 3' UTR; N line promoter not specified in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plants compared under identical sterile tissue culture conditions; third youngest leaf sampled at same developmental stage
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic/transplastomic expression; western blot for Flag-SQS; metabolite profiling by GC/MS and UHPLC/MS/MS; phenotyping; RNA-seq
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (GEO: GSE74103); metabolomics by GC/MS and UHPLC/MS/MS
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Small curled leaves, clustered inflorescences, aborted buds, reduced pollen and fertility, widespread changes in unrelated metabolites and nuclear transcripts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes ~150-fold squalene accumulation in tobacco but also provokes strong off-target metabolic and transcriptomic reprogramming; SQS-only transplastomic plants also altered nearly 100 metabolites including strong nicotine increase in PH background
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SQS overexpression from nuclear and/or chloroplast genomes strongly increased squalene accumulation in tobacco. 2. Chloroplast expression produced thousands-fold more SQS protein than nuclear expression, but squalene levels remained similar across engineered lines, indicating non-SQS bottlenecks. 3. SQS engineering triggered severe developmental defects and broad off-target effects on metabolome and transcriptome, including nicotine elevation in an SQS-only PH line.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good direct engineering target for squalene production, but deployment requires balancing precursor supply and creating storage sinks such as trichomes or lipid droplets to reduce pleiotropy
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Source gene was a codon-optimized Flag-tagged yeast SQS; authors infer metabolite-mediated retrograde/anterograde signalling from pathway imbalance rather than squalene amount alone
